introSection:
* "Rock n' Roller Coaster" redirects here. You may have been looking for Rock 'n' Roller Coaster.

Canyon Blaster is a steel roller coaster manufactured by Arrow Development located at Six Flags Great Escape in Queensbury, New York, USA. It previously operated at Opryland USA in Nashville, Tennessee, USA from the park's opening in 1972 to the park's closure in 1997.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
In 1972, Timber Topper opened with Opryland USA in Nashville, Tennessee, USA and was the first roller coaster at the park. It was located in the Music of Today area. For 1978, it was renamed Rock n' Roller Coaster when the area became known as Do Wah Diddy Area.

After the closure of Opryland USA in 1997, many rides, including Rock n' Roller Coaster, were purchased by Six Flags (then known as Premier Parks) and placed at the site of Old Indiana Fun-n-Water Park in Thorntown, Indiana, USA, a property Six Flags planned to develop. In July 2002, the property was sold off with most of the rides scrapped and removed from the site in 2003 and Rock n' Roller Coaster was removed and shipped off in late 2002.

In November 2002, track pieces of Rock n' Roller Coaster were spotted on site at The Great Escape & Splashwater Kingdom in Queensbury, New York, USA. The park subsequently filed a permit for the roller coaster.

In February 2003, Six Flags officially announced that the roller coaster would be rebuilt at the park. It would be located in the Ghost Town area. It reopened on June 27, 2003 as Canyon Blaster, replacing the former Ghost Town Railroad and Tornado rides. On May 23, 2013, the park announced for the 2013 season that the ride would go backwards for a limited time.

sections.title: Color scheme
sections.content: While it was at Opryland USA, Rock n' Roller Coaster was painted grey. As part of its transition into Canyon Blaster, it was repainted red with light brown supports. Some of the track was replaced as part of the move, the newer sections have curved ties.

sections.title: External links
sections.content:
* Canyon Blaster on the parks website
 * Rock n' Roller Coaster on the parks website

fullText:
* "Rock n' Roller Coaster" redirects here. You may have been looking for Rock 'n' Roller Coaster.

Canyon Blaster is a steel roller coaster manufactured by Arrow Development located at Six Flags Great Escape in Queensbury, New York, USA. It previously operated at Opryland USA in Nashville, Tennessee, USA from the park's opening in 1972 to the park's closure in 1997.

In 1972, Timber Topper opened with Opryland USA in Nashville, Tennessee, USA and was the first roller coaster at the park. It was located in the Music of Today area. For 1978, it was renamed Rock n' Roller Coaster when the area became known as Do Wah Diddy Area.

After the closure of Opryland USA in 1997, many rides, including Rock n' Roller Coaster, were purchased by Six Flags (then known as Premier Parks) and placed at the site of Old Indiana Fun-n-Water Park in Thorntown, Indiana, USA, a property Six Flags planned to develop. In July 2002, the property was sold off with most of the rides scrapped and removed from the site in 2003 and Rock n' Roller Coaster was removed and shipped off in late 2002.

In November 2002, track pieces of Rock n' Roller Coaster were spotted on site at The Great Escape & Splashwater Kingdom in Queensbury, New York, USA. The park subsequently filed a permit for the roller coaster.

In February 2003, Six Flags officially announced that the roller coaster would be rebuilt at the park. It would be located in the Ghost Town area. It reopened on June 27, 2003 as Canyon Blaster, replacing the former Ghost Town Railroad and Tornado rides. On May 23, 2013, the park announced for the 2013 season that the ride would go backwards for a limited time.



While it was at Opryland USA, Rock n' Roller Coaster was painted grey. As part of its transition into Canyon Blaster, it was repainted red with light brown supports. Some of the track was replaced as part of the move, the newer sections have curved ties.
